title: Your First Python Program
subtitle: Learn to display text, use strings, and write comments
learning_objectives: Use the print() function to display text and numbers on screen
Understand the difference between single and double quotes for strings
Print multiple items in a single print() call
Write comments in your code using the # symbol

sections.heading: The print() Function — Your Main Way to Show Output
sections.content:
The most important tool you will use in your early Python journey is the print() function. It tells Python: "Display this on the screen."

To use it, write print, then open parentheses (, put what you want to show, and close with ).

Text that you want to display must be wrapped in quotation marks — this tells Python that you are giving it a piece of text (called a string). You can use either single quotes 'like this' or double quotes "like this" — both work exactly the same way. Choose whichever you prefer, but be consistent!

You can also print numbers directly without any quotes — Python understands numbers natively.
sections.code_example:
print("Hello, World!")
print('This uses single quotes')
print(42)
print(3.14)
sections.code_output:
Hello, World!
This uses single quotes
42
3.14
sections.note: Single quotes and double quotes are interchangeable in Python for creating strings. Most style guides prefer double quotes, but either is perfectly fine.

sections.heading: Printing Multiple Things at Once
sections.content:
You can print more than one item in a single print() call by separating them with commas. Python will display them with a space between each one automatically.

This is really handy when you want to combine text and numbers or show several pieces of information on the same line.
sections.code_example:
print("My name is", "Alex")
print("I am", 25, "years old")
print("Pi is approximately", 3.14159)
sections.code_output:
My name is Alex
I am 25 years old
Pi is approximately 3.14159
sections.note: Notice that Python automatically adds a space between items separated by commas. You don't need to add spaces yourself!

sections.heading: Comments — Notes for Humans, Ignored by Python
sections.content:
As your programs grow, it becomes important to explain what your code is doing and why. That's where comments come in.

A comment starts with a # (hash symbol). Everything after the # on that line is completely ignored by Python — it is only there for you (and other humans) to read.

Comments are incredibly useful for:
  • Explaining what a piece of code does
  • Leaving yourself reminders
  • Temporarily disabling a line of code
Good programmers write comments regularly. It makes code much easier to understand later!
sections.code_example:
# This program greets the user
# Written by a Python beginner

print("Welcome to my program!")  # This prints a greeting
print("Let's learn Python together!")
# print("This line won't run because it is commented out")
sections.code_output:
Welcome to my program!
Let's learn Python together!
sections.note: You can put a comment at the end of a line of code (called an inline comment), or on its own line. Both are perfectly valid.

exercises.title: The Classic Hello, World!
exercises.prompt: Every programming course starts with the same tradition: printing "Hello, World!" to the screen. Use print() to display exactly: Hello, World!
hints: Put the text inside quotation marks inside the parentheses.
Make sure you include the comma and exclamation mark: Hello, World!

exercises.title: Introduce Yourself
exercises.prompt:
Use print() to display your name on the screen. For example, if your name is Sam, print: My name is Sam

You can use your own name or any name you like!
hints: Use print() with your text inside quotes.
Start your message with: My name is

exercises.title: Comments and Two Lines
exercises.prompt:
Write a small program that does the following:
1. Add a comment at the top explaining what the program does (start the line with #)
2. Print: Learning Python is fun!
3. Print: I wrote my first program!
exercises.starter_code:
# Your comment here

# Print two lines below
exercises.solution:
# This program prints two encouraging messages
print("Learning Python is fun!")
print("I wrote my first program!")
hints: A comment starts with # and Python ignores it completely.
You need two separate print() calls for two lines of output.
Make sure your output matches exactly: Learning Python is fun! and I wrote my first program!

tests.message: Output contains: Learning Python is fun!
Output contains: I wrote my first program!

quiz.question: What does Python do with a line that starts with #?
options: It runs it as a special system command
It displays it on the screen in bold
It completely ignores it — it is a comment for humans only
It causes an error because # is not allowed
quiz.explanation: Lines starting with # are comments. Python completely ignores them when running the code. Comments are only there to help humans read and understand the code.

quiz.question:
What will this code print?
print("I am", 10, "years old")
options: I am10years old
I am, 10, years old
I am 10 years old
Error — you cannot mix text and numbers in print()
quiz.explanation: When you separate items with commas in print(), Python automatically adds a single space between each one. So print("I am", 10, "years old") outputs: I am 10 years old

quiz.question: Which of the following correctly prints the number 42?
options: print("42")
print(42)
Both work — both print 42 on the screen
Neither works — you need to use display() instead
quiz.explanation: Both print("42") and print(42) display 42 on the screen. The difference is the type: "42" is a string (text) and 42 is an integer (number). For just displaying it, the result looks the same.

quiz.question: What is a comment in Python?
options: A line of code that runs a special hidden command
A line starting with # that Python completely ignores — it's for humans to read
A message that gets printed to the screen automatically
A type of error message
quiz.explanation: A comment starts with # and is completely ignored by Python when it runs. Comments are notes for you and other humans reading the code — they have no effect on what the program does.

quiz.question: Can you use both single quotes and double quotes to create a string in Python?
options: No — you must always use double quotes
No — you must always use single quotes
Yes — both work exactly the same way
Only double quotes work for strings with numbers inside
quiz.explanation: In Python, single quotes and double quotes are completely interchangeable for creating strings. 'Hello' and "Hello" are identical. The main reason to choose one over the other is when your text itself contains a quote character.

completion_message: You just wrote your first real Python programs — and you're already using print(), strings, numbers, and comments like a pro! These are skills you will use in every single program you ever write. Brilliant work — on to the next lesson!
